------

 

 

 

 

 

![A logo for a company  AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

 

**FlightDeck 
** Agentic Control Plane

Product Overview

March 2026

 

**Danny Kosarin
** Founder, Builder, Solopreneur

 

 

 

 

**
**

# The Product

### The Premise: The Founder as Solopreneur

If you’re reading this, you’re probably one of three things:

a founder contemplating developing your own app for the first time;

a founder already struggling with developing your own app; or

an experienced developer coming up against organization, governance, and context window issues that *no existing tool actually solves*.



If you are a founder trying to build a software product by yourself — or just getting the nerve to try — you’re probably dealing with one of two nightmares. If you’re already comfortable with AI doing a lot of the heavy lifting, you know the real problem is not that it can’t write code, it’s that it can’t remember what you want, what you said, and what you meant from one day to the next. Each session is like starting over with a new employee on their first day of work. They know roughly how to do the job. They just don’t know what the job is, or how you want it done.

And if you’re new to all of this, the problem is even more primal: Terminal looks scary, command lines feel dangerous, and the whole idea of a software stack made up of products you’ve never even heard of — let alone understand what they’re for and how they fit into the process — feels like a world built for people more technical than you.

If you are already developing but coming up against common AI development limitations — the context getting stale, the tools not talking to each other, the governance falling apart as your project grows — this is still the product for you.

FlightDeck is for all three of you. And everyone in between.

FlightDeck is an AI-guided product-building system for solo founders and solopreneurs who use AI to build software and may lack the advanced coding skills traditionally needed to build software on their own. And to be clear, it’s not just the AI in the code builder, it’s with you at every step of your journey. It’s an AI integrated, intelligence-guided pipeline from 3 AM bolt-upright-in-bed inspiration to a shipped app, and beyond. 



It exists to give the solo founder a place to wrap their arms and their brain around the whole project. Not just the plan, not just the code, not just the tasks, but the whole living system. And not just one project. FlightDeck manages multiple projects simultaneously, each one fully isolated — its own codebase, its own database, its own deployment, its own governance — so you can switch between projects without anything bleeding into anything else.

## The First Step: What Most Builders Miss

Most “vibe coders” start near the end of the process. They just start vibing and coding without a clear vision of what they’re building, let alone why and for whom. This is like pulling the arm on a slot machine in Vegas and hoping it will build you a functioning business that attracts customers and makes you lots of money. What are the chances of that? Zero.

AI inference, and therefore coding development, is by definition a probabilistic process. The less you specify, the less likely you are to get what you actually want or need. That’s why successful companies and the apps they are built around start with a plan. Research. Understanding the marketplace, customers, and competitors. Positioning documents. Financial realities. Governing principles. Real thinking before random output. Real planning instead of slop.

FlightDeck helps you start the right way, because after all, you’re not just building an app. You’re building a business.

FlightDeck is designed to push a probabilistic process as far as possible toward deterministic results. Give the system enough information, invariants, guidelines, and guardrails, and you dramatically increase the odds of getting what you actually imagined — instead of whatever the machine happened to feel like giving you that day.

Here’s a real example of how that works:

Most developer tools show a document’s health using a colored dot next to a filename — green, yellow, red. That’s the industry standard. But then? Good luck, figure it out yourself. But, early in FlightDeck’s development, we wrote a governance rule — one of the constitutional invariants that govern the entire system. It says a warning without an explanation is a product failure. The system must never report a problem without telling you what is wrong, why it matters, and what to do about it, in plain language, with a button to fix it whenever possible.

 

That single rule, written during planning before the feature was ever built, completely changed how the health indicator had to be designed. Instead of a red dot that says “File not found,” FlightDeck can say something like: “This file probably exists on your computer but hasn’t been pushed to GitHub yet. Push and refresh.” And then give you the button right there. Forget AI doing that. A lot of human developers wouldn’t even build it that way unprompted. It is more work. It requires more thought, more fallback checks and edge cases to worry about, and just a lot more planning and care. But because the rule existed in the system before the feature was built, the AI helping build it knew the standard it had to meet.

That is what governance does. It turns your preferences, standards, and non-negotiables into constraints that shape every feature before you have to ask for them again.

## The Real Problem: AI Can’t Remember and You Might Forget



Each and every AI tool you use — ChatGPT, Claude, any of them — works inside something called a context window. That’s the amount of information the AI can hold in its head during a single conversation and sends to the LLM with every message and prompt. When that window fills up, or when you close the chat and start a new one, everything you discussed is gone. Any work product like a markdown file artifact that wasn’t explicitly saved somewhere outside that conversation — a file committed to your repo, a document saved to your system — may be gone with it.

The AI doesn’t know what your product is. It doesn’t know what you decided last session. It doesn’t know your architecture, your rules, your priorities, or your name. And worse, if you don’t religiously close chats and start new ones, your AI agent starts to silently fail. This means you will offer you obviously erroneous information or worse, quietly erroneous hallucinations that you can’t know are wrong until it’s too late.

It’s like the movie *50 First Dates*. Every single session.

So, you sit down to work, and the first hour is spent re-explaining your entire project to a tool that helped you build it yesterday. What are we building? What’s the tech stack? What decisions did we already make? What files matter? What did we ship last time? You become a human clipboard, copying and pasting context back and forth into a machine that has no memory of you.

And it gets worse with every tool you add. There is no system connecting them. There is no memory spanning them. There is just you, trying to hold it all together in your head. *This is why you need a control plane.*

FlightDeck solves this problem by turning your thinking into governed documents — structured, classified, version-tracked files that the system manages and protects on your behalf — and those documents become the persistent memory that AI tools cannot create on their own.

## How It Starts: Flight School

When you create a new project in FlightDeck, the system doesn’t just ask you to name it and start typing. It walks you through Flight School — a deep, AI-guided intake process that captures everything the system needs to know about both your project and, more importantly, you. Flight School has two parts:

**The first part is the FlightDeck Intake Questionnaire** that learns how you work. Not just what you’re building, but how you think. What’s your tolerance for risk? How do you want AI to talk to you — direct and blunt, or careful and consultative? What systems are off-limits? What’s your experience level? Do you prefer to review everything before it ships, or do you trust the system to handle routine changes? What tools do you already use? The questions offer multiple choice answers, and even teach you concepts and terminology in the process. By the end of the intake questionnaire, you will already feel more equipped for the job ahead, and not a single line of code has been written.

From your answers, the system generates two real governance documents. The first is *SOUL.md* — and that name is not an accident. It is designed to capture your entrepreneurial spirit, foundational principles, identity, values, and operating style as a founder. The second is *CLAUDE.md*, which becomes the behavioral contract your AI agents follow in every session — style preferences, design rules, invariants, and boundaries. These are not preferences buried in a settings page. They are actual files that get loaded into your AI tools every time they start working, so the AI knows who you are and how you expect to be treated — before you say a word.

**The second part of Flight School is the Project Creation Questionnaire** — an AI-guided setup process that captures everything about the specific product you want to build. What is it and what is it called? Who is it for? Where does the code live? What database does it use? What cloud services power it? What’s the deployment target? What AI tools will you use to build it? What surface or surfaces is it intended for? Every answer becomes a structured record that FlightDeck uses to configure the project, set up your AI crew, and, critically, inject the right context into every AI session automatically.

Based on your answers, the system even tells you what you need to get started — what kind of setup makes sense for what you’re building. Just planning to use the web version? A browser is all you need. Building something more ambitious with local AI agents running code on your machine? Here’s exactly what hardware and software you’ll want, and why. FlightDeck recommends, it doesn’t gatekeep. You can always start small and grow into more power later.

**The result:** next time you sit down to work, FlightDeck already knows who you are, how you work, what your product is, what you decided, what you shipped, and what you planned to do next. It rehydrates your depleted AI tools with the right context from your governed documents so you never start from zero again. Your specifications, your decisions, your architectural constraints, and your planning documents are not just files sitting in a folder. They are the persistent memory layer that makes AI actually useful across sessions, across tools, and across the life of a product.

That is the core of FlightDeck. Not just AI help. Governed AI help through a specific development pipeline, with memory, structure, lineage, and continuity.

## The Pipeline: From Idea to Shipped Product

FlightDeck brings all of that into one governed system, and it guides you through a clear pipeline from thinking to shipping.

It starts where every product starts — with an idea. Maybe it hits you at 3 AM. FlightDeck has a capture mode for that. You can grab an idea from your phone, any device, any time, without touching your codebase, without risking anything, and without needing any technical setup. It just saves to the system. It sits in the inbox until you review it and promote it to a real planning document through the normal process.

When you’re ready to think seriously, FlightDeck walks you through turning that idea into a real plan: what are you building, who it is for, what are the rules, what matters most. It helps you create the governing documents that define your product and your company — not just feature lists, but the underlying principles, boundaries, strategic decisions, and structural thinking that keep the whole thing from drifting into chaos as it grows.

And the system doesn’t encourage you to create documents for the sake of creating documents. FlightDeck prefers that you search for existing plans, specs, and decisions before writing new ones. Reuse or iterate on what’s already been thought through. Build on what exists. Create something new only when something new is actually needed. It’s a thinking system, not a document factory.

From there, your plans become specifications — clear, detailed descriptions of what each part of the product should do. Specifications are not just documentation. In FlightDeck, a specification is a commitment: if it exists, it will be decomposed into executable work. Those specifications become **flight plans** — FlightDeck’s term for the individual work items that actually get built, each one permanently linked back to the spec that created it. And those flight plans get organized into focused sprints — short, bounded work sessions with a clear goal — so you always know what to work on next, what’s already done, and what’s still waiting.

Every piece of work in the system traces back to the specification that created it. Every specification traces back to the plan that shaped it. Nothing floats loose. Nothing gets lost. And nothing fails silently — when something goes wrong, FlightDeck tells you what happened, why it matters, and what to do about it. In plain language. With a button to fix it.

You can always answer the question: Why are we building this? Where did the idea come from? And what happened to it?

## Not Just Documents — Artifacts

FlightDeck doesn’t just manage text files. It manages everything your product is made of.

Most tools treat a wireframe — the visual blueprint that shows what a screen of your app will look like — as a static image you export from a design tool and paste into a slide deck. In FlightDeck, wireframes are live, functional components that actually run inside the system. They’re written in JSX — the same language your app is built in — and they render right inside the control plane as interactive, working previews. You can see your app taking shape with real layout, real color palettes, and real interactive elements, right alongside the specification that describes what it should do and the flight plan that tracks the work to build it. You don’t leave the system to see what your product looks like. You see it where you plan it, and it stays linked to a planning document and an execution plan, just like everything else in the system.

The system even knows how to handle different types of content automatically. Drop in a markdown document and it gets classified, summarized by AI, and reviewed through the inbox. Drop in a JSX wireframe and it renders as a live, interactive preview. Drop in a logo, a screenshot, an animation, or an architecture diagram and it gets ingested with the right metadata — dimensions, format, file size — and displayed with a thumbnail you can click to see full-size. You don’t have to tell FlightDeck what kind of file you’re giving it. It figures that out and handles each type the way it should be handled.

And none of these artifacts float loose. Every wireframe is linked to the specification it illustrates. Every logo is part of the brand assets governed by the manifest. Every diagram traces back to the planning document or architectural decision that created it. When you’re reviewing the spec for your login screen, the wireframe for that screen, the color palette it uses, and the work items to build it are all connected inside the same system. Design, planning, and execution live together — not scattered across Figma, Google Drive, Notion, and your desktop.

As your product grows, so does the artifact library — animations, design tokens (the locked color palettes and typography rules that keep your product visually consistent), icon sets, and any other creative asset that’s part of what you’re shipping. Everything is versioned, everything is findable, and everything traces back to the decision that created it.

## The Cockpit Metaphor

The whole system uses the metaphor of aviation, and it’s not decoration — it’s how the product thinks.

You’re the pilot in command. Your AI tools are your crew.

Your first officer is the system intelligence — a bring-your-own-API hook to an LLM of your choice, providing Overwatch-level system intelligence and guiding you through every process. With it, you can see the status of everything in the system, and you can ask for reports and audits that list your documents and the health of the connections between the actual files in the repo folder with the database lines that summarize, define and link them together. You can get a report that summarizes your entire project and the app you’re developing based solely on the documents you have created and ingested into the system. This can be a really good test of whether your vision will be understood by AI — because if it can’t, it can’t be built into a viable product that reflects that vision.

Then there are your crew members: systems like ChatGPT for architecture and strategic thinking, Claude for reasoning, analysis, and document creation, Claude Code for writing and modifying actual source code, and others as needed. Each one has a defined job, clear limits, and rules about what it can and can’t do without your sign-off.

The system is bring-your-own-AI, so it’s intelligence-agnostic — meaning your entire development stack is up to you. You can use ChatGPT, Claude, Claude Code, Codex, or any product you like. We recommend using multiple AI products together — round-tripping ideas between them in an iterative process adds an extra layer of refinement and catches mistakes no single tool would find on its own. But if you don’t know what to choose, or what these things even mean, Overwatch has you covered and will recommend or specify exactly what you need based on the information you provide in natural English during Flight School.

FlightDeck is your cockpit and operating system: the instrument panel that shows you where everything stands, what needs attention, and what’s ready to go. It connects to your tech stack — the collection of services that power your product, like GitHub (where your code lives), Vercel (where your app gets deployed to the internet), and Supabase (your database and user authentication backend) — and helps coordinate them from one place. You do not need to understand every piece of that stack on day one. FlightDeck introduces and explains each part as it becomes relevant to what you are building.

## Six Surfaces, One System

Your cockpit has six main areas, each with a distinct job:

**Cockpit —** your at-a-glance command deck. Are you ready to work? What needs your attention right now? What’s the current state of the project? What reports can I generate to understand my project better? This is where you start every session and where cautions surface when something in the system needs a decision.

**Flight Planning —** your execution board. Every specification you’ve written, with the work items derived from it, organized by completion status. This is where you see the truth chain: this spec produced these tasks, these are done, these are in progress, these are waiting.

**Flight Logs —** the historical memory of the system. Session history, receipts, prior decisions, old ideas, archived past work, and eventually the history of your conversations and dispatches with your AI crew. This is where you go when you need to know what happened before now. No more “what was I doing last Tuesday?”

**Document Manifest —** your document and artifact library. Every plan, specification, policy, design document, wireframe, and visual asset in the system — classified, searchable, and health-checked. Every document has a live health indicator: green means it exists and everything matches, yellow means something has drifted, red means there’s a problem. And when it’s red, the system doesn’t just show you a colored dot — it tells you what’s wrong, why it happened, and gives you a button to fix it.

**Crew Manifest —** your AI team roster. Which AI tools are connected, what each one has been asked to do, what they’ve produced, and the full history of your dispatches and conversations with them.

**Maintenance —** your service hangar. Diagnostic tools, security settings, system preferences, provider bindings, key rotation, and the machinery you need to keep the system healthy and under control. This is where you fix and maintain the system itself.

## Flight Mode and Developer Mode

FlightDeck knows that not every founder needs the same level of guidance.

If you’re new to building software products — or new to the AI tools that FlightDeck coordinates — the product runs in Flight Mode. Everything is labeled in plain language. Mouseover and collapsible tooltips explain what things mean. The system uses aviation metaphors and founder-friendly terminology throughout: cautions instead of error codes, destinations instead of milestones, refueling instead of context rehydration.

If you’re an experienced developer who already knows what a git commit is and doesn’t need the hand-holding, one toggle switches the entire product into Developer Mode. The cheat sheets disappear. The labels switch to technical terminology. The interface gets out of your way and lets you work at full speed. Same system, same data, same governance — just without the training wheels.

You can switch between modes at any time. They are presentation layers, not different products.

## The Web Product — and What’s Coming

Everything described above is the web-based version of FlightDeck. You open it in a browser. It works on any device. It handles planning, document governance, specification management, work tracking, and project oversight.

*But the web version is the planning and review cockpit.* The full execution cockpit — the part where things really get built — is coming in the desktop application, and that’s where FlightDeck becomes something that doesn’t exist anywhere else.

The desktop version adds live terminal windows — the text-based interfaces where code actually runs — where your AI agents work in real time. You can watch Claude Code write and modify your source code as it happens. You can see your development server running. You can monitor your console output and git history as changes land. You can even set up two or more AI intelligences to collaborate directly with each other in what we call a dogfight — a back-and-forth iterative review process with the sole purpose of developing the very best solution to a given problem you have posed, then turning it into a design document, which becomes a specification, which ultimately becomes a feature or an app.

**Most importantly, the desktop version includes Overwatch** — a real-time coordination layer that sits between you and your AI agents during active building sessions. When Claude Code is about to execute something significant — a database migration (a structural change to how your data is stored), a file deletion, a major refactor (reorganizing how the code is structured) — and asks for your approval, Overwatch helps you understand what’s being proposed, what the risks are, and what your options are. It’s the difference between an AI asking “Should I run this migration?” and you having no idea what that means, versus FlightDeck showing you exactly what will change, what could go wrong, and recommending whether to approve, reject, or ask for more information.

Overwatch exists because the most dangerous moment in AI-assisted development isn’t when the AI writes bad code. It’s when the AI asks you a question you don’t understand, and you say yes because you don’t want to look stupid. FlightDeck makes sure you never have to make a decision you don’t understand.

**The desktop version also includes the Operations Deck** — a tabbed panel with dedicated stations for each part of your active build environment: your AI agent’s workspace, your console and git output, your development server, and a live preview of your running application. Full situational awareness during execution, not just planning and review.

The web version ships first. It’s where you think, plan, specify, and track. The desktop version follows. It’s where you build, execute, and ship — with the same governance, the same lineage, and the same system of truth underneath.

## What FlightDeck Is Not

**FlightDeck is not a project management tool.** Those are built for teams of humans coordinating with each other. FlightDeck is built for one human coordinating AI agents.

**It is not a chatbot with a nicer interface.** Chatbots help you *talk* to AI. FlightDeck governs, tracks, and audits what AI *does* on your behalf — so you can trust what’s being built and prove how every decision was made.

**And it is not a deployment tool that only helps after all the hard decisions have already been made.** FlightDeck is where decisions get made in the first place — and then tracked, all the way through to a shipped product.

## The Bet

The bet behind FlightDeck is simple: one founder, with the right structure and the right AI tools working inside a governed system, can build and ship a real software product with the speed and discipline of a small company. Not by replacing human judgment with AI, but by giving that founder a system where their thinking is captured, their decisions are organized, and their execution is tracked — instead of scattered across a dozen disconnected tools and forgotten chat windows.

FlightDeck is for founders who don’t just want help writing code. It’s for founders who want help building a product, running a company, and keeping the whole thing together as it grows.

** Agentic Control Plane

Product Overview

March 2026

 

**Danny Kosarin
** Founder, Builder, Solopreneur

 

 

 

 

**
**

# The Product

### The Premise: The Founder as Solopreneur

If you’re reading this, you’re probably one of three things:

a founder contemplating developing your own app for the first time;

a founder already struggling with developing your own app; or

an experienced developer coming up against organization, governance, and context window issues that *no existing tool actually solves*.

If you are a founder trying to build a software product by yourself — or just getting the nerve to try — you’re probably dealing with one of two nightmares. If you’re already comfortable with AI doing a lot of the heavy lifting, you know the real problem is not that it can’t write code, it’s that it can’t remember what you want, what you said, and what you meant from one day to the next. Each session is like starting over with a new employee on their first day of work. They know roughly how to do the job. They just don’t know what the job is, or how you want it done.

And if you’re new to all of this, the problem is even more primal: Terminal looks scary, command lines feel dangerous, and the whole idea of a software stack made up of products you’ve never even heard of — let alone understand what they’re for and how they fit into the process — feels like a world built for people more technical than you.

If you are already developing but coming up against common AI development limitations — the context getting stale, the tools not talking to each other, the governance falling apart as your project grows — this is still the product for you.

FlightDeck is for all three of you. And everyone in between.

FlightDeck is an AI-guided product-building system for solo founders and solopreneurs who use AI to build software and may lack the advanced coding skills traditionally needed to build software on their own. And to be clear, it’s not just the AI in the code builder, it’s with you at every step of your journey. It’s an AI integrated, intelligence-guided pipeline from 3 AM bolt-upright-in-bed inspiration to a shipped app, and beyond. 

It exists to give the solo founder a place to wrap their arms and their brain around the whole project. Not just the plan, not just the code, not just the tasks, but the whole living system. And not just one project. FlightDeck manages multiple projects simultaneously, each one fully isolated — its own codebase, its own database, its own deployment, its own governance — so you can switch between projects without anything bleeding into anything else.

## The First Step: What Most Builders Miss

Most “vibe coders” start near the end of the process. They just start vibing and coding without a clear vision of what they’re building, let alone why and for whom. This is like pulling the arm on a slot machine in Vegas and hoping it will build you a functioning business that attracts customers and makes you lots of money. What are the chances of that? Zero.

AI inference, and therefore coding development, is by definition a probabilistic process. The less you specify, the less likely you are to get what you actually want or need. That’s why successful companies and the apps they are built around start with a plan. Research. Understanding the marketplace, customers, and competitors. Positioning documents. Financial realities. Governing principles. Real thinking before random output. Real planning instead of slop.

FlightDeck helps you start the right way, because after all, you’re not just building an app. You’re building a business.

FlightDeck is designed to push a probabilistic process as far as possible toward deterministic results. Give the system enough information, invariants, guidelines, and guardrails, and you dramatically increase the odds of getting what you actually imagined — instead of whatever the machine happened to feel like giving you that day.

Here’s a real example of how that works:

Most developer tools show a document’s health using a colored dot next to a filename — green, yellow, red. That’s the industry standard. But then? Good luck, figure it out yourself. But, early in FlightDeck’s development, we wrote a governance rule — one of the constitutional invariants that govern the entire system. It says a warning without an explanation is a product failure. The system must never report a problem without telling you what is wrong, why it matters, and what to do about it, in plain language, with a button to fix it whenever possible.

 

That single rule, written during planning before the feature was ever built, completely changed how the health indicator had to be designed. Instead of a red dot that says “File not found,” FlightDeck can say something like: “This file probably exists on your computer but hasn’t been pushed to GitHub yet. Push and refresh.” And then give you the button right there. Forget AI doing that. A lot of human developers wouldn’t even build it that way unprompted. It is more work. It requires more thought, more fallback checks and edge cases to worry about, and just a lot more planning and care. But because the rule existed in the system before the feature was built, the AI helping build it knew the standard it had to meet.

That is what governance does. It turns your preferences, standards, and non-negotiables into constraints that shape every feature before you have to ask for them again.

## The Real Problem: AI Can’t Remember and You Might Forget

Each and every AI tool you use — ChatGPT, Claude, any of them — works inside something called a context window. That’s the amount of information the AI can hold in its head during a single conversation and sends to the LLM with every message and prompt. When that window fills up, or when you close the chat and start a new one, everything you discussed is gone. Any work product like a markdown file artifact that wasn’t explicitly saved somewhere outside that conversation — a file committed to your repo, a document saved to your system — may be gone with it.

The AI doesn’t know what your product is. It doesn’t know what you decided last session. It doesn’t know your architecture, your rules, your priorities, or your name. And worse, if you don’t religiously close chats and start new ones, your AI agent starts to silently fail. This means you will offer you obviously erroneous information or worse, quietly erroneous hallucinations that you can’t know are wrong until it’s too late.

It’s like the movie *50 First Dates*. Every single session.

So, you sit down to work, and the first hour is spent re-explaining your entire project to a tool that helped you build it yesterday. What are we building? What’s the tech stack? What decisions did we already make? What files matter? What did we ship last time? You become a human clipboard, copying and pasting context back and forth into a machine that has no memory of you.

And it gets worse with every tool you add. There is no system connecting them. There is no memory spanning them. There is just you, trying to hold it all together in your head. *This is why you need a control plane.*

FlightDeck solves this problem by turning your thinking into governed documents — structured, classified, version-tracked files that the system manages and protects on your behalf — and those documents become the persistent memory that AI tools cannot create on their own.

## How It Starts: Flight School

When you create a new project in FlightDeck, the system doesn’t just ask you to name it and start typing. It walks you through Flight School — a deep, AI-guided intake process that captures everything the system needs to know about both your project and, more importantly, you. Flight School has two parts:

**The first part is the FlightDeck Intake Questionnaire** that learns how you work. Not just what you’re building, but how you think. What’s your tolerance for risk? How do you want AI to talk to you — direct and blunt, or careful and consultative? What systems are off-limits? What’s your experience level? Do you prefer to review everything before it ships, or do you trust the system to handle routine changes? What tools do you already use? The questions offer multiple choice answers, and even teach you concepts and terminology in the process. By the end of the intake questionnaire, you will already feel more equipped for the job ahead, and not a single line of code has been written.

From your answers, the system generates two real governance documents. The first is *SOUL.md* — and that name is not an accident. It is designed to capture your entrepreneurial spirit, foundational principles, identity, values, and operating style as a founder. The second is *CLAUDE.md*, which becomes the behavioral contract your AI agents follow in every session — style preferences, design rules, invariants, and boundaries. These are not preferences buried in a settings page. They are actual files that get loaded into your AI tools every time they start working, so the AI knows who you are and how you expect to be treated — before you say a word.

**The second part of Flight School is the Project Creation Questionnaire** — an AI-guided setup process that captures everything about the specific product you want to build. What is it and what is it called? Who is it for? Where does the code live? What database does it use? What cloud services power it? What’s the deployment target? What AI tools will you use to build it? What surface or surfaces is it intended for? Every answer becomes a structured record that FlightDeck uses to configure the project, set up your AI crew, and, critically, inject the right context into every AI session automatically.

Based on your answers, the system even tells you what you need to get started — what kind of setup makes sense for what you’re building. Just planning to use the web version? A browser is all you need. Building something more ambitious with local AI agents running code on your machine? Here’s exactly what hardware and software you’ll want, and why. FlightDeck recommends, it doesn’t gatekeep. You can always start small and grow into more power later.

**The result:** next time you sit down to work, FlightDeck already knows who you are, how you work, what your product is, what you decided, what you shipped, and what you planned to do next. It rehydrates your depleted AI tools with the right context from your governed documents so you never start from zero again. Your specifications, your decisions, your architectural constraints, and your planning documents are not just files sitting in a folder. They are the persistent memory layer that makes AI actually useful across sessions, across tools, and across the life of a product.

That is the core of FlightDeck. Not just AI help. Governed AI help through a specific development pipeline, with memory, structure, lineage, and continuity.

## The Pipeline: From Idea to Shipped Product

FlightDeck brings all of that into one governed system, and it guides you through a clear pipeline from thinking to shipping.

It starts where every product starts — with an idea. Maybe it hits you at 3 AM. FlightDeck has a capture mode for that. You can grab an idea from your phone, any device, any time, without touching your codebase, without risking anything, and without needing any technical setup. It just saves to the system. It sits in the inbox until you review it and promote it to a real planning document through the normal process.

When you’re ready to think seriously, FlightDeck walks you through turning that idea into a real plan: what are you building, who it is for, what are the rules, what matters most. It helps you create the governing documents that define your product and your company — not just feature lists, but the underlying principles, boundaries, strategic decisions, and structural thinking that keep the whole thing from drifting into chaos as it grows.

And the system doesn’t encourage you to create documents for the sake of creating documents. FlightDeck prefers that you search for existing plans, specs, and decisions before writing new ones. Reuse or iterate on what’s already been thought through. Build on what exists. Create something new only when something new is actually needed. It’s a thinking system, not a document factory.

From there, your plans become specifications — clear, detailed descriptions of what each part of the product should do. Specifications are not just documentation. In FlightDeck, a specification is a commitment: if it exists, it will be decomposed into executable work. Those specifications become **flight plans** — FlightDeck’s term for the individual work items that actually get built, each one permanently linked back to the spec that created it. And those flight plans get organized into focused sprints — short, bounded work sessions with a clear goal — so you always know what to work on next, what’s already done, and what’s still waiting.

Every piece of work in the system traces back to the specification that created it. Every specification traces back to the plan that shaped it. Nothing floats loose. Nothing gets lost. And nothing fails silently — when something goes wrong, FlightDeck tells you what happened, why it matters, and what to do about it. In plain language. With a button to fix it.

You can always answer the question: Why are we building this? Where did the idea come from? And what happened to it?

## Not Just Documents — Artifacts

FlightDeck doesn’t just manage text files. It manages everything your product is made of.

Most tools treat a wireframe — the visual blueprint that shows what a screen of your app will look like — as a static image you export from a design tool and paste into a slide deck. In FlightDeck, wireframes are live, functional components that actually run inside the system. They’re written in JSX — the same language your app is built in — and they render right inside the control plane as interactive, working previews. You can see your app taking shape with real layout, real color palettes, and real interactive elements, right alongside the specification that describes what it should do and the flight plan that tracks the work to build it. You don’t leave the system to see what your product looks like. You see it where you plan it, and it stays linked to a planning document and an execution plan, just like everything else in the system.

The system even knows how to handle different types of content automatically. Drop in a markdown document and it gets classified, summarized by AI, and reviewed through the inbox. Drop in a JSX wireframe and it renders as a live, interactive preview. Drop in a logo, a screenshot, an animation, or an architecture diagram and it gets ingested with the right metadata — dimensions, format, file size — and displayed with a thumbnail you can click to see full-size. You don’t have to tell FlightDeck what kind of file you’re giving it. It figures that out and handles each type the way it should be handled.

And none of these artifacts float loose. Every wireframe is linked to the specification it illustrates. Every logo is part of the brand assets governed by the manifest. Every diagram traces back to the planning document or architectural decision that created it. When you’re reviewing the spec for your login screen, the wireframe for that screen, the color palette it uses, and the work items to build it are all connected inside the same system. Design, planning, and execution live together — not scattered across Figma, Google Drive, Notion, and your desktop.

As your product grows, so does the artifact library — animations, design tokens (the locked color palettes and typography rules that keep your product visually consistent), icon sets, and any other creative asset that’s part of what you’re shipping. Everything is versioned, everything is findable, and everything traces back to the decision that created it.

## The Cockpit Metaphor

The whole system uses the metaphor of aviation, and it’s not decoration — it’s how the product thinks.

You’re the pilot in command. Your AI tools are your crew.

Your first officer is the system intelligence — a bring-your-own-API hook to an LLM of your choice, providing Overwatch-level system intelligence and guiding you through every process. With it, you can see the status of everything in the system, and you can ask for reports and audits that list your documents and the health of the connections between the actual files in the repo folder with the database lines that summarize, define and link them together. You can get a report that summarizes your entire project and the app you’re developing based solely on the documents you have created and ingested into the system. This can be a really good test of whether your vision will be understood by AI — because if it can’t, it can’t be built into a viable product that reflects that vision.

Then there are your crew members: systems like ChatGPT for architecture and strategic thinking, Claude for reasoning, analysis, and document creation, Claude Code for writing and modifying actual source code, and others as needed. Each one has a defined job, clear limits, and rules about what it can and can’t do without your sign-off.

The system is bring-your-own-AI, so it’s intelligence-agnostic — meaning your entire development stack is up to you. You can use ChatGPT, Claude, Claude Code, Codex, or any product you like. We recommend using multiple AI products together — round-tripping ideas between them in an iterative process adds an extra layer of refinement and catches mistakes no single tool would find on its own. But if you don’t know what to choose, or what these things even mean, Overwatch has you covered and will recommend or specify exactly what you need based on the information you provide in natural English during Flight School.

FlightDeck is your cockpit and operating system: the instrument panel that shows you where everything stands, what needs attention, and what’s ready to go. It connects to your tech stack — the collection of services that power your product, like GitHub (where your code lives), Vercel (where your app gets deployed to the internet), and Supabase (your database and user authentication backend) — and helps coordinate them from one place. You do not need to understand every piece of that stack on day one. FlightDeck introduces and explains each part as it becomes relevant to what you are building.

## Six Surfaces, One System

Your cockpit has six main areas, each with a distinct job:

**Cockpit —** your at-a-glance command deck. Are you ready to work? What needs your attention right now? What’s the current state of the project? What reports can I generate to understand my project better? This is where you start every session and where cautions surface when something in the system needs a decision.

**Flight Planning —** your execution board. Every specification you’ve written, with the work items derived from it, organized by completion status. This is where you see the truth chain: this spec produced these tasks, these are done, these are in progress, these are waiting.

**Flight Logs —** the historical memory of the system. Session history, receipts, prior decisions, old ideas, archived past work, and eventually the history of your conversations and dispatches with your AI crew. This is where you go when you need to know what happened before now. No more “what was I doing last Tuesday?”

**Document Manifest —** your document and artifact library. Every plan, specification, policy, design document, wireframe, and visual asset in the system — classified, searchable, and health-checked. Every document has a live health indicator: green means it exists and everything matches, yellow means something has drifted, red means there’s a problem. And when it’s red, the system doesn’t just show you a colored dot — it tells you what’s wrong, why it happened, and gives you a button to fix it.

**Crew Manifest —** your AI team roster. Which AI tools are connected, what each one has been asked to do, what they’ve produced, and the full history of your dispatches and conversations with them.

**Maintenance —** your service hangar. Diagnostic tools, security settings, system preferences, provider bindings, key rotation, and the machinery you need to keep the system healthy and under control. This is where you fix and maintain the system itself.

## Flight Mode and Developer Mode

FlightDeck knows that not every founder needs the same level of guidance.

If you’re new to building software products — or new to the AI tools that FlightDeck coordinates — the product runs in Flight Mode. Everything is labeled in plain language. Mouseover and collapsible tooltips explain what things mean. The system uses aviation metaphors and founder-friendly terminology throughout: cautions instead of error codes, destinations instead of milestones, refueling instead of context rehydration.

If you’re an experienced developer who already knows what a git commit is and doesn’t need the hand-holding, one toggle switches the entire product into Developer Mode. The cheat sheets disappear. The labels switch to technical terminology. The interface gets out of your way and lets you work at full speed. Same system, same data, same governance — just without the training wheels.

You can switch between modes at any time. They are presentation layers, not different products.

## The Web Product — and What’s Coming

Everything described above is the web-based version of FlightDeck. You open it in a browser. It works on any device. It handles planning, document governance, specification management, work tracking, and project oversight.

*But the web version is the planning and review cockpit.* The full execution cockpit — the part where things really get built — is coming in the desktop application, and that’s where FlightDeck becomes something that doesn’t exist anywhere else.

The desktop version adds live terminal windows — the text-based interfaces where code actually runs — where your AI agents work in real time. You can watch Claude Code write and modify your source code as it happens. You can see your development server running. You can monitor your console output and git history as changes land. You can even set up two or more AI intelligences to collaborate directly with each other in what we call a dogfight — a back-and-forth iterative review process with the sole purpose of developing the very best solution to a given problem you have posed, then turning it into a design document, which becomes a specification, which ultimately becomes a feature or an app.

**Most importantly, the desktop version includes Overwatch** — a real-time coordination layer that sits between you and your AI agents during active building sessions. When Claude Code is about to execute something significant — a database migration (a structural change to how your data is stored), a file deletion, a major refactor (reorganizing how the code is structured) — and asks for your approval, Overwatch helps you understand what’s being proposed, what the risks are, and what your options are. It’s the difference between an AI asking “Should I run this migration?” and you having no idea what that means, versus FlightDeck showing you exactly what will change, what could go wrong, and recommending whether to approve, reject, or ask for more information.

Overwatch exists because the most dangerous moment in AI-assisted development isn’t when the AI writes bad code. It’s when the AI asks you a question you don’t understand, and you say yes because you don’t want to look stupid. FlightDeck makes sure you never have to make a decision you don’t understand.

**The desktop version also includes the Operations Deck** — a tabbed panel with dedicated stations for each part of your active build environment: your AI agent’s workspace, your console and git output, your development server, and a live preview of your running application. Full situational awareness during execution, not just planning and review.

The web version ships first. It’s where you think, plan, specify, and track. The desktop version follows. It’s where you build, execute, and ship — with the same governance, the same lineage, and the same system of truth underneath.

## What FlightDeck Is Not

**FlightDeck is not a project management tool.** Those are built for teams of humans coordinating with each other. FlightDeck is built for one human coordinating AI agents.

**It is not a chatbot with a nicer interface.** Chatbots help you *talk* to AI. FlightDeck governs, tracks, and audits what AI *does* on your behalf — so you can trust what’s being built and prove how every decision was made.

**And it is not a deployment tool that only helps after all the hard decisions have already been made.** FlightDeck is where decisions get made in the first place — and then tracked, all the way through to a shipped product.

## The Bet

The bet behind FlightDeck is simple: one founder, with the right structure and the right AI tools working inside a governed system, can build and ship a real software product with the speed and discipline of a small company. Not by replacing human judgment with AI, but by giving that founder a system where their thinking is captured, their decisions are organized, and their execution is tracked — instead of scattered across a dozen disconnected tools and forgotten chat windows.

FlightDeck is for founders who don’t just want help writing code. It’s for founders who want help building a product, running a company, and keeping the whole thing together as it grows.
